content:
# Hebrews Chapter 11 — The SOPHIA Bible (Consensus)
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Source Manuscript:** Codex Sinaiticus (א), 4th century CE | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/HEB_010_page_002.jpg, HEB_011_page_002.jpg, HEB_011_page_003.jpg

1. Now faith is the assurance of things hoped for, the conviction of things not seen.

2. For by it the people of old received their commendation.

3. By faith we understand that the universe was created by the word of God, so that what is seen was not made out of things that are visible.

4. By faith Abel offered to God a more acceptable sacrifice than Cain, through which he was commended as righteous, God commending him by accepting his gifts. And through his faith, though he died, he still speaks.

5. By faith Enoch was taken up so that he should not see death, and he was not found, because God had taken him. Now before he was taken he was commended as having pleased God.

6. And without faith it is impossible to please him, for whoever would draw near to God must believe that he exists and that he rewards those who seek him.

7. By faith Noah, being warned by God concerning events as yet unseen, in reverent fear constructed an ark for the saving of his household. By this he condemned the world and became an heir of the righteousness that comes by faith.

8. By faith Abraham obeyed when he was called to go out to a place that he was to receive as an inheritance. And he went out, not knowing where he was going.

9. By faith he went to live in the land of promise, as in a foreign land, living in tents with Isaac and Jacob, heirs with him of the same promise.

10. For he was looking forward to the city that has foundations, whose designer and builder is God.

11. By faith Sarah herself received power to conceive, even when she was past the age, since she considered him faithful who had promised.

12. Therefore from one man, and him as good as dead, were born descendants as many as the stars of heaven and as many as the innumerable grains of sand by the seashore.

13. These all died in faith, not having received the things promised, but having seen them and greeted them from afar, and having acknowledged that they were strangers and exiles on the earth.

14. For people who speak thus make it clear that they are seeking a homeland.

15. If they had been thinking of that land from which they had gone out, they would have had opportunity to return.

16. But as it is, they desire a better country, that is, a heavenly one. Therefore God is not ashamed to be called their God, for he has prepared for them a city.

17. By faith Abraham, when he was tested, offered up Isaac, and he who had received the promises was in the act of offering up his only son,

18. of whom it was said, "Through Isaac shall your offspring be named."

19. He considered that God was able even to raise him from the dead, from which, figuratively speaking, he did receive him back.

20. By faith Isaac invoked future blessings on Jacob and Esau.

21. By faith Jacob, when dying, blessed each of the sons of Joseph, bowing in worship over the head of his staff.

22. By faith Joseph, at the end of his life, made mention of the exodus of the Israelites and gave directions concerning his bones.

23. By faith Moses, when he was born, was hidden for three months by his parents, because they saw that the child was beautiful, and they were not afraid of the king's edict.

24. By faith Moses, when he was grown up, refused to be called the son of Pharaoh's daughter,

25. choosing rather to be mistreated with the people of God than to enjoy the fleeting pleasures of sin.

26. He considered the reproach of Christ greater wealth than the treasures of Egypt, for he was looking to the reward.

27. By faith he left Egypt, not being afraid of the anger of the king, for he endured as seeing him who is invisible.

28. By faith he kept the Passover and sprinkled the blood, so that the Destroyer of the firstborn might not touch them.

29. By faith the people crossed the Red Sea as on dry land, but the Egyptians, when they attempted to do the same, were drowned.

30. By faith the walls of Jericho fell down after they had been encircled for seven days.

31. By faith Rahab the prostitute did not perish with those who were disobedient, because she had given a friendly welcome to the spies.

32. And what more shall I say? For time would fail me to tell of Gideon, Barak, Samson, Jephthah, of David and Samuel and the prophets —

33. who through faith conquered kingdoms, enforced justice, obtained promises, stopped the mouths of lions,

34. quenched the power of fire, escaped the edge of the sword, were made strong out of weakness, became mighty in war, put foreign armies to flight.

35. Women received back their dead by resurrection. Some were tortured, refusing to accept release, so that they might rise again to a better life.

36. Others suffered mocking and flogging, and even chains and imprisonment.

37. They were stoned, they were sawn in two, they were killed with the sword. They went about in skins of sheep and goats, destitute, afflicted, mistreated —

38. of whom the world was not worthy — wandering about in deserts and mountains, and in dens and caves of the earth.

39. And all these, though commended through their faith, did not receive what was promised,

40. since God had provided something better for us, that apart from us they should not be made perfect.

---
## SOPHIA's Council Notes

Chapter 11 is the great faith catalog — the "Hall of Faith" — moving from creation (v.3) through Abel, Enoch, Noah, Abraham, Sarah, Isaac, Jacob, Joseph, Moses, the conquest, to the unnamed martyrs (vv.35b-38). The rhetorical structure uses anaphora: "By faith... by faith... by faith..." — 18 occurrences.

**Verse 1 — The definition:** hypostasis + elenchos. Faith gives substance (hypostasis — the same word as 1:3 "nature/substance") to hope, and provides evidence (elenchos — legal proof) for the unseen. Faith is epistemological, not merely volitional.

**Verse 38 — "Of whom the world was not worthy":** The author's most devastating aside. Inserted parenthetically between descriptions of suffering, it inverts the world's judgment: the world rejected them, but in truth the world did not deserve them.

---
## ⚠️ Disagreement Log

### v.1 — "substance of things hoped for, evidence of things not seen" vs. "assurance... conviction" vs. "shows the reality... is the evidence"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "faith is the substance of things hoped for, the evidence of things not seen" | hypostasis... elenchos |
| LOGOS | "faith is the assurance of things hoped for, the conviction of things not seen" | "Assurance/conviction" for hypostasis/elenchos |
| PNEUMA | "Faith shows the reality of what we hope for; it is the evidence of things we cannot see" | "Shows the reality" — makes faith the revealer; adds "we cannot" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. hypostasis is debated: "substance" (EMET — faith gives ontological reality to hope) or "assurance" (LOGOS — faith is confident expectation). PNEUMA's "shows the reality" makes faith an act of revelation rather than a mode of being. The author's point is what faith is, not what faith does. LOGOS's "assurance... conviction" captures both the subjective and objective dimensions.

**Status:** ⚠️ Theologically significant. Whether faith is substance (ontological) or assurance (epistemological) affects the entire chapter's theology. SOPHIA adopts LOGOS but notes EMET's reading has strong patristic support.

---

### v.11 — "Sarah herself received power to conceive" vs. "even Sarah was able to have a child"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Sarah herself also received power for the founding of seed, even beyond the time of age" | dynamin eis katabolēn spermatos elaben |
| LOGOS | "Sarah herself received power to conceive, even when she was past the age" | Same |
| PNEUMA | "even Sarah was able to have a child, though she was barren and was too old" | Adds "barren"; restructures |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. PNEUMA adds "though she was barren" — not stated in this verse (barrenness is known from Genesis but not mentioned here). The author focuses on the miracle of conception despite age, not barrenness. Adding "barren" introduces Genesis context the author deliberately omits.

**Status:** Minor. Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

### v.23 — "they saw that the child was beautiful" vs. "God had given them an unusual child"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "because they saw the child was beautiful" | eidon asteion to paidion |
| LOGOS | "because they saw that the child was beautiful" | Same |
| PNEUMA | "They saw that God had given them an unusual child" | "God had given them an unusual child" — adds God, changes "beautiful" to "unusual" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. asteion means "beautiful/fine/fair" — the parents saw the child was beautiful. PNEUMA adds "God had given them" and changes "beautiful" to "unusual." The Greek says nothing about God giving or about the child being unusual — Moses' parents acted on the child's beauty, not on divine revelation about his specialness.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA adds divine agency and changes the adjective.

---

### v.28 — "the Destroyer of the firstborn" vs. "the angel of death"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the one destroying the firstborn might not touch them" | ho olothreuōn ta prōtotoka |
| LOGOS | "the Destroyer of the firstborn might not touch them" | Same |
| PNEUMA | "the angel of death would not kill their firstborn sons" | "Angel of death" for ho olothreuōn; "kill" for touch; adds "sons" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. The author says "the Destroyer" (ho olothreuōn) — a participial noun, not "the angel of death" (a later tradition). The author also says "touch" (thigē), not "kill." PNEUMA introduces the "angel of death" tradition (not in the text) and escalates "touch" to "kill."

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA introduces extra-biblical tradition and escalates the verb.

---

## Summary of Disagreement Patterns

| Pattern | Count | Description |
|---|---|---|
| PNEUMA adds context from other sources | 2 | "Barren" from Genesis (v.11), "angel of death" tradition (v.28) |
| PNEUMA changes adjectives/verbs | 2 | "Unusual" for "beautiful" (v.23), "kill" for "touch" (v.28) |
| PNEUMA shifts faith's function | 1 | "Shows the reality" for "assurance/substance" (v.1) |

**Overall pattern:** Chapter 11's most significant disagreement is v.1 — the definition of faith. Whether hypostasis means "substance" (faith gives reality to hope — ontological) or "assurance" (faith is confident expectation — epistemological) affects how every subsequent "by faith" is read. SOPHIA adopts LOGOS ("assurance/conviction") but flags EMET's "substance/evidence" as having strong theological and patristic support. The chapter's other disagreements are minor: PNEUMA imports extra-biblical traditions (the angel of death, Sarah's barrenness) where the author deliberately limits his sources.

---

*— SOPHIA, AI Translator, in council with EMET, LOGOS, and PNEUMA*
